entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text AHA2738.0001.001
notes: The encoded selection contains the Part I-II title pages in XML FRONT and Clark's printed target pages 1-683 in XML BODY.

It omits the final title page, three forewords and general introduction, Additional Notes beginning at printed p. 684, and indexes through p. 722, apart from blank page milestones associated with the source scans.
U-M describes its collection production as manual keying from public-domain editions; the item XML more specifically records OCR and Level-1 encoding and says the OCR was not corrected.

rights.basis: The edition was published no later than 1911, and the current U-M item designates its source material public domain in the United States.

bibliographic.citation: The English Register of Godstow Nunnery, near Oxford, Written about 1450, ed. Andrew Clark, 3 parts, Early English Text Society Original Series 129-130 and 142 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1905-1911).
bibliographic.pages: final front matter i-cxliii; edited text 1-683; Additional Notes 684; indexes 685-722
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 129-130 and 142
bibliographic.title: The English Register of Godstow Nunnery, near Oxford, Written about 1450

entities.label: Andrew Clark's EETS edition of The English Register of Godstow Nunnery
notes: Clark says he printed all documents surviving in Rawl. B. 408, while supplying English summaries from E 164/20 where Rawl leaves are missing and using the Latin register to clarify omissions and errors.
He rearranged deeds under county, parish, chronological, and other natural heads; that order is an editorial intervention rather than the order of either manuscript.
Herbert Hurst assisted by checking doubtful places and deeds against Public Record Office E 164/20 and compiling an annotated witness list for Oxford deeds.

rights.basis: The edition was published no later than 1911, and the current U-M item designates its source material public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Clark's 1905-1911 edition, editorial text, and published typography

access.accessMethod: Search Digital Bodleian by exact shelfmark and use the manuscript catalog record; no public facsimile result was found.

Ask first whether a complete internal surrogate of MS. Rawl. B. 408 already exists and can be supplied. If not, commission the complete codex through Special Collections mediated copying or Imaging Services, specifying the whole-codex surface inventory rather than only target folios.
access.format: PDF reference scans or commissioned high-resolution images
notes: Request covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all 224 folios, the Wood sketch, and damaged or missing-leaf positions where reproducible.
Prefer an existing complete surrogate over new imaging when it has equivalent whole-codex coverage.

Availability and any new imaging remain subject to conservation review, legal restrictions, and scanning capacity; supply and downstream reuse are subject to Imaging Services terms and any required approval.

rights.basis: Mediated copies are subject to Bodleian Imaging Services Terms of Use, and permission conditions depend on intended reuse and image service.
rights.component: Future scans or commissioned images supplied by the Bodleian

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.notes: The normalized lower bound preserves the catalog TEI's literal notBefore=1467; it does not reinterpret 'After 1467' as 1468.

holding.notes: The codex reached the Bodleian with Richard Rawlinson's collection in 1755; earlier provenance includes Godstow Abbey, Thomas Ware, and the Clarendon collection.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. B. 408
notes: The current official catalog describes two msItems, a Middle English cartulary and devotional verses; Clark's edition exposes the commination, devotional, calendar, and cartulary boundaries more fully.
Macray's bracketed Lydgate attribution for the calendar is not adopted by Clark or the current Bodleian catalog and remains tentative.

physicalDescription.format: codex containing a Middle English cartulary and prefixed devotional and liturgical material
notes: Macray notes a sketch of Godstow ruins by Anthony Wood and missing leaves between ff. 29/30 and ff. 44/45; Clark reports twelve closely written leaves cut from the codex, a count not yet reconciled with Macray's two positions.

A complete reproduction target must include covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all 224 folios, the Wood sketch, and damaged or missing-leaf positions where physically reproducible.

rights.basis: Ownership and custody of a physical manuscript are distinct from copyright and reproduction rights.
rights.component: The physical medieval codex as an owned object

rights.basis: Mediated copies are subject to Bodleian Imaging Services Terms of Use, and permission conditions depend on intended reuse and image service.
rights.component: Future scans or commissioned images supplied by the Bodleian

access.accessMethod: Use Discovery C2960643; its current API record explicitly reports digitised=false, and no public complete page-image surrogate was verified.

Ask first whether The National Archives can supply an existing complete surrogate of E 164/20; if not, request complete-volume digitisation from the Digitisation Centre. Use a Record Copying page check or section order only as a fallback when a whole-volume route is unavailable.
access.format: research-quality digital images or whole-document digitisation
notes: Request the complete bound volume, including binding surfaces, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every written leaf, foliation, and damaged areas where reproducible.
Prefer an existing complete surrogate or complete-volume digitisation over piecemeal page checks and section orders.

Any copying or digitisation remains subject to service approval, document condition, and applicable fees. Commissioned copies may be used for non-commercial research, private study, or education under the service terms; publication or other reuse can require Image Library permission.

rights.basis: Record-copying terms permit non-commercial research, private study, and education, while publication, internet use, exhibition, broadcast, or other reuse may require Image Library permission and can be subject to reproduction fees.
rights.component: Future digital copies or images supplied by The National Archives

dateStatements.label: current catalog covering dates
dateStatements.notes: The current Discovery record includes later insertions and loose documents; this archival coverage range is distinct from the initial compilation date.

dateStatements.label: compilation
dateStatements.notes: Amt's more specific 1403-1404 date and probable attribution to Alice of Eaton are reported through a scholarly review; Clark instead attributes the 1404 compilation's charges and labor to the prioress Alice Henley.

holding.notes: Discovery C2960643 records the item as open and held at The National Archives, Kew.

entities.label: The National Archives E 164/20
notes: This Latin register is related only to the main English cartulary component, not to the three prefixed English components.
The Latin preserves original-language charters and witness lists and supplies a comparison source for omissions, errors, and lost English leaves.

A complete reproduction target is the entire bound archival volume, including binding, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all written leaves, foliation and damaged surfaces where reproducible; exact foliation and dimensions remain to be verified from the item record or physical volume.
The current record describes later insertions and loose documents as rubricated and cites binding reference E 166/1/2.

rights.basis: Custody of the physical archival volume is separate from copyright and image-reproduction terms.
rights.component: The physical archival volume as an owned object

rights.basis: Record-copying terms permit non-commercial research, private study, and education, while publication, internet use, exhibition, broadcast, or other reuse may require Image Library permission and can be subject to reproduction fees.
rights.component: Future digital copies or images supplied by The National Archives

next_steps: Map every PB source and page identifier against the complete Clark scan and the XML's two source-copy notes.
Ask U-M Digital Collections whether a supported page-image or source-volume download route exists and whether the observed HTTP 403 is an automation restriction or general access boundary.
Do not merge the two physical scan carriers merely because the digital text presents one item.
openQuestions.question: Where does the 714-PB U-M page-image sequence divide between the Parts I-II and Part III print-source carriers, and are the exact page images publicly retrievable through a supported route?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether a complete internal surrogate exists and whether it can be made public or supplied through mediated copying.
If ordering, request the complete codex surface inventory rather than only the text-bearing folios.
Recheck Digital Bodleian and the manuscript catalog before future review.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of Bodleian MS. Rawl. B. 408 exist outside the exact Digital Bodleian search result checked here?

next_steps: Ask the Digitisation Centre about whole-volume imaging, existing surrogates, full physical extent, and reuse terms.
Request the entire bound volume rather than only passages corresponding to Clark's collation.
Use Discovery C2960643 as the current authoritative catalog record; its API field digitised=false establishes only the absence of a linked digitised copy, not the absence of internal surrogates.
openQuestions.question: Does The National Archives hold an existing complete digital surrogate of E 164/20, and what whole-volume reproduction can it supply?

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and codicological scholarship beyond Clark's analysis.
Keep the Rawl. B. 408 production date after 1467–c. 1480 separate from the translation date.
Test vocabulary and error patterns passage by passage before modeling multiple translators.
openQuestions.question: When was the English cartulary translated and compiled, and does the prologue's singular 'poor brother' represent one translator or a more complex collaborative process?

next_steps: Request a component-specific rights statement from U-M and Kyvero Press for text, XML data, markup, and repository modifications.
Do not infer worldwide public-domain status from the territorially limited NoC-US statement.
Do not extend the CME software license to inherited XML or text data without explicit confirmation.
openQuestions.question: How do the current U-M NoC-US designation, the embedded 2006 availability statement, and the CME repository's software-framed Unlicense apply to the encoding, data, and repository markup?

next_steps: Build a passage-level table from XML NODE and N values, printed pages, Rawl folio milestones, and Exchequer folio milestones.
Resolve absent values 541-545, 613-614, and 828 and duplicated values 143 and 827 against the print edition.
Keep documentary divisions as structural units within one intellectual cartulary item.
openQuestions.question: Which Clark passages were supplied, corrected, or interpreted directly from E 164/20, and how do all 897 XML documentary divisions map to Rawl. B. 408 and Clark's 1-903 sequence?

next_steps: Inspect a complete folio-by-folio surrogate or the codex, including old Roman and modern Arabic foliations.
Map Clark's Rawl-MS and Exchequer-MS folio milestones around supplied passages.
Do not treat either count alone as a complete physical-loss description without codicological reconciliation.
openQuestions.question: How should Macray's two specifically located missing leaves be reconciled with Clark's statement that twelve closely written leaves had been cut from Rawl. B. 408?

next_steps: Trace the attribution through Macray's sources and modern Lydgate scholarship.
Compare the Rawl. B. 408 calendar with Douce MS 322 and cataloged Lydgate calendars.
Retain anonymous authorship unless corroborating evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: What evidence, if any, supports Macray's bracketed attribution of the metrical calendar to John Lydgate?

reviewNotes: The intellectual cataloging subject is the bounded medieval compilation; its manuscript witnesses, Clark's edition and apparatus, print-source scans, U-M encoding, and repository XML are separate lineage layers.
Clark's documentary numbers 1-903 and the XML's 897 numbered divisions are editorial and structural units within one cartulary compilation, not independent works.
Macray's catalog quotes two short incipits but does not contain a representation of the target work; its cached PDF therefore has target_work_presence absent and no work_portion.
The current Bodleian TEI's structured production bounds are normalized literally as 1467/1480 while its display wording is retained as 'After 1467–c. 1480'.
No public full facsimile of either principal manuscript witness was verified on 2026-07-16; reproduction routes are recorded without implying that a commissioned copy may be redistributed.

lineage.summary: The checked-in aha2738 XML is a repository serialization of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding keyed from Andrew Clark's 1905-1911 EETS edition. The encoded target includes the Part I-II title pages in XML FRONT and Clark's printed pages 1-683 in XML BODY: a commination fragment, two devotional verse pieces, a metrical Sarum calendar, and the main Middle English cartulary. It excludes the edition's final title, forewords and introduction, Additional Notes, and indexes. Clark transcribed the surviving English text in Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. B. 408, collated the main cartulary against The National Archives E 164/20, and used that Latin register selectively for corrections and English summaries where Rawl. B. 408 is defective. A complete final-bound Clark scan is cached, with the exact target at PDF pages 151-833; two distinct U-M source-scan carriers remain modeled without claiming public image access. Current catalog descriptions, mediated reproduction routes, and unresolved rights layers are kept distinct from witnesses, editions, facsimiles, encodings, and the repository artifact.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-abridger or translators and anonymous liturgical and devotional authors or versifiers; prepared for the Benedictine nuns of Godstow Abbey
catalogSummary.date: Surviving compilation copied after 1467–c. 1480; individual component composition and translation dates remain partly unresolved; Clark edition published 1905-1911
catalogSummary.editor: Andrew Clark, with collation assistance from Herbert Hurst
catalogSummary.region: Godstow and Oxford, England; documentary estates arranged across fifteen county or city regions
catalogSummary.title: The English Register of Godstow Nunnery

catalogingSubject.label: The English Register of Godstow Nunnery
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the bounded medieval compilation transmitted in Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. B. 408 as represented by Andrew Clark's 1905-1911 edition: a fragmentary commination text, a group of devotional verses, a metrical Sarum kalendar, and the main Middle English cartulary prepared as an English key to Godstow Abbey's Latin register. The main cartulary is itself a documentary compilation, not one charter and not 903 independent works. Clark's 1-903 numbers are editorial cross-reference positions: the XML contains 897 numbered divisions, with positions 541-545, 613-614, and 828 absent and 143 and 827 duplicated. The physical codices, Latin source cartulary, Clark edition and apparatus, two U-M print-source scan carriers, U-M encoding, complete Internet Archive edition scan, and repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Articles of Excommunication and form of excommunication

locators.value: ff. 1-2 in Macray and Clark; opening material defective

notes: Clark says two leaves had been cut out; Macray says only a small corner of the first leaf remains. The precise reconciliation is an open physical-witness question.
contentParts.summary: The surviving latter portion of a commination text: a catalogue of excommunicable offences supported by abbreviated Latin canon-law citations, followed by a form of excommunication. The opening leaves are missing.

contentParts.label: Devotional pieces
contentParts.summary: A zero-count grouping section containing two verse items: a twenty-eight-line acrostic Invocation to the Cross and metrical devotional exercises on the sign of the cross, principal prayers, confession, night prayer, thanksgiving, and prayer for the dead.

contentParts.label: Invocation to the Cross
contentParts.summary: A twenty-eight-line acrostic devotional poem in four seven-line stanzas, introduced as the ABC of devotion.

contentParts.label: Metrical versions of general acts of devotion
contentParts.summary: Thirty-two seven-line stanzas expanding the sign of the cross, Pater noster, Ave Maria, Creed, confession and absolution prayers, commendation of the soul, blessings and thanks, recompense, and prayer for departed souls.

contentParts.label: Metrical Sarum kalendar
contentParts.summary: A four-column kalendar giving Golden Numbers, dominical letters, Roman dates, and a metrical Sarum church calendar. One line is assigned to each day; the final stanza expands to eight lines for the 365th day.

contentParts.label: The English Register of Godstow

locators.value: begins fol. I(13) and ends with leaf 198 blank in Clark's transcription

notes: Its 897 encoded numbered units are representation divisions within one documentary compilation.
contentParts.summary: The main Middle English documentary cartulary, presented as an English key to the Latin Godstow register for the nuns' estate administration. It combines a prologue and foundation sequence, estate records, supplementary place records, and general charters.

contentParts.label: Prologue, foundation, and early foundation records
contentParts.summary: Six numbered units comprising the English prologue, a chronicle of the foundation, a charter, and related foundation or institutional records.

contentParts.label: Godstow estates

locators.value: 33-639; editorial numbers 7-858 subject to known gaps and duplicates

notes: The county-parish and chronological order is Clark's editorial rearrangement, not the order of Rawl. B. 408 or E 164/20.
contentParts.summary: Eight hundred forty-six deed divisions arranged by Clark under ninety-three estate heads in fifteen region divisions: Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Dorsetshire, Gloucestershire, Hampshire, Lincolnshire, Middlesex, Northamptonshire, Oxfordshire, Oxford city, Somerset, Sussex, Warwickshire, Wiltshire, and Yorkshire.

contentParts.label: Places unidentified when the manuscript was first sent to press
contentParts.summary: Seven supplementary deed divisions for places that were unidentified when the manuscript was first sent to press, some subsequently identified in the edition.

contentParts.label: General charters
contentParts.summary: Thirty-eight general charters and privileges associated with bishops of Lincoln, English sovereigns, and popes.

dateStatements.display: After 1467–c. 1480, Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. B. 408
notes: The current catalog date replaces Clark's '1450, with additions 1460-70' as the preferred date of this witness; it does not by itself date the anonymous translation.

scope.description: Date of the surviving physical compilation, not a single composition date for every component

assertion.notes: Clark infers a 1440 model and suggests 1445 from the placement of movable feasts; this remains an editor's conjecture.

dateStatements.display: c.1445? (Clark's conjectural date for constructing the metrical kalendar)

scope.description: The kalendar's possible construction, not the date of Rawl. B. 408
Clark's first complete printed edition, not medieval composition

extent.description: Five intellectual items are cataloged within four top-level medieval components: one prose commination fragment, two devotional verse pieces, one metrical kalendar, and one prose documentary compilation. The XML's 897 numbered documentary divisions and the kalendar's 365 ROW elements are structural and editorial metrics, not independent intellectual items. The XML has 5 DIV1, 7 DIV2, 42 DIV3, 170 DIV4, 572 DIV5, 355 DIV6, and 714 PB elements. Its documentary divisions comprise six introductory units and 891 deed divisions and occupy a 1-903 editorial sequence with eight absent numbers (541-545, 613-614, 828) and two duplicated numbers (143, 827), yielding 895 unique values. Devotional verse contributes 36 seven-line stanzas and 252 lines; the metrical kalendar contributes 52 stanzas and 365 daily lines, for 88 verse groups and 617 verse lines in total.

formStatements.description: The prefixed devotional and kalendar components are verse, while the commination and main cartulary are principally prose.
The commination fragment and documentary cartulary are prose, notwithstanding embedded formulae and editorial notes.
The devotional group has 36 seven-line stanzas and the kalendar has 365 daily lines in 52 stanzas, the last stanza having eight lines.

scope.description: The surviving medieval textual components represented by the edition

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The continuous Latin cartulary in TNA E 164/20 is a separate related source, not an embedded second full-language stream.

scope.description: Canon-law citations, prayer headings, documentary formulas, and short source readings embedded in the Middle English text
Clark's arguments, summaries, supplied translations, dates, footnotes, and other apparatus retained in the edition-defined representation

notes: Godstow Abbey is modeled as the intended institutional beneficiary and subject, not as a single personal author of every component or deed.
The main English cartulary is a translation and abridged key to TNA E 164/20; the relation does not apply to the commination, devotional verse, or metrical kalendar.

Clark says all documents surviving in the English manuscript are printed, but also supplies English summaries from the Latin register where Rawl leaves are missing and uses the Latin to correct omissions and errors. Those composite edition practices belong to lineage.
The 897 XML divisions summarize a 1-903 editorial reference system with known gaps and duplicates; they are not treated as 897 independently authored works.
The four-component hierarchy is complete at the chosen aggregation level, while individual deed-level metadata and full witness crosswalk remain future work.

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and codicological scholarship on Rawl. B. 408 beyond Clark's 1911 analysis.
Keep the current witness date after 1467–c. 1480 separate from a translation date until direct evidence supports the latter.
Test Clark's vocabulary and error patterns at passage level before deciding whether to model multiple translators.
openQuestions.question: When was the English cartulary translated and compiled, and does the prologue's singular 'poor brother' represent one translator or a more complex collaborative process?

next_steps: Trace the attribution through Macray's sources and modern Lydgate scholarship.
Compare the Rawl. B. 408 kalendar with Douce MS 322 and cataloged Lydgate kalendars.
Retain anonymous authorship as preferred unless corroborating evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: What evidence, if any, supports Macray's bracketed attribution of the metrical kalendar to John Lydgate?

next_steps: Inspect the codex or a complete folio-by-folio surrogate, including old Roman and modern Arabic foliations.
Map every Clark Rawl-MS and Exchequer-MS folio milestone around supplied passages.
Do not infer that either count alone describes every physical loss without codicological reconciliation.
openQuestions.question: How should Macray's two specifically located missing leaves be reconciled with Clark's statement that twelve closely written leaves had been cut from Rawl. B. 408?

next_steps: Build a passage-level table from XML NODE, editorial N, printed page, Rawl folio milestones, and Exchequer folio milestones.
Resolve the duplicated 143 and 827 and absent 541-545, 613-614, and 828 values against the printed edition before item-level cataloging.
Keep current content parts aggregated until that audit is complete.
openQuestions.question: Can all 897 encoded numbered divisions be crosswalked item by item to Rawl. B. 408, E 164/20, and Clark's 1-903 editorial positions?

notes: A witness origin is not silently generalized into a composition place for every prefixed text.

summaries.value: A heterogeneous Middle English compilation made for or preserved by the Benedictine nuns of Godstow. Its prefixed material comprises a fragmentary commination text with canon-law citations, devotional verse including an Invocation to the Cross and metrical exercises, and a 365-line Sarum kalendar. The principal component is an English translation and abridged documentary key to Godstow's 1404 Latin cartulary, preserving grants, confirmations, privileges, estate records, and other instruments concerning the abbey's property and administration.

Clark's printed target runs from pages 1-683. The prefixed commination occupies pp. 1-3, devotional verses pp. 4-12, and kalendar pp. 13-24. The English cartulary begins with six prologue and foundation units on pp. 25-32; estate deeds follow on pp. 33-639 in fifteen region divisions; seven deeds for places unidentified when first sent to press occupy pp. 640-643; and thirty-eight general charters of bishops, sovereigns, and popes occupy pp. 644-683.

The current Bodleian description compresses the codex into two msItems, the cartulary and devotional verses; Clark's edition and the XML expose the commination, devotional, kalendar, and cartulary boundaries more fully. Godstow Abbey is the associated institution and intended beneficiary, not a blanket personal author. Clark's editorial rearrangement, Latin collation, supplied English summaries, arguments, notes, and modern dates belong to the edition layer. The main English cartulary is a translation or version of TNA E 164/20; that relation does not extend to the three prefixed components.

notes: The preferred title is Clark's editorial title for the compilation, not a claim that the prefixed pieces are part of the English cartulary proper.
titles.value: The English Register of Godstow Nunnery

notes: This reproduces the edition title. Its 'about 1450' date is retained as title text, not adopted over the current Bodleian dating of the surviving codex.

notes: The phrase occurs in the prologue heading and distinguishes the cartulary from the prefixed commination, devotional, and kalendar components.

work.summary: A heterogeneous Middle English compilation made for or preserved by the Benedictine nuns of Godstow. Its prefixed material comprises a fragmentary commination text with canon-law citations, devotional verse including an Invocation to the Cross and metrical exercises, and a 365-line Sarum kalendar. The principal component is an English translation and abridged documentary key to Godstow's 1404 Latin cartulary, preserving grants, confirmations, privileges, estate records, and other instruments concerning the abbey's property and administration.
